Your $2,100/month rent at Eastside Flats is actually quite reasonable for Austin's current market — recent data shows 1-bedroom apartments averaging around $1,350/month citywide, so you're paying a premium, but it's not dramatically out of range for a nicer or more centrally located unit.
The real question for a freelancer isn't just "can I afford this?" — it's "can I afford this in my worst months?"
How your rent stacks up against your income
Your May income came in at $13,960 across three client payments (Figma Design Systems at $8,500, Notion UX Research at $4,200, and Stripe at $1,260). That's a strong month. But looking at your full cash flow history, January through April shows zero recorded income — meaning your data only captures one active month so far.
With that single strong month as reference, your $2,100 rent represents about 15% of May's gross income. Under the standard 30% guideline, that looks comfortable. But freelance income is lumpy by nature, and a month like May doesn't tell the whole story.
Here's the key stress test: if you landed, say, $4,000–$5,000 in a slower month, your rent alone would consume 42–52% of income before taxes, software subscriptions, healthcare, groceries, or anything else. That's where the risk lives.
What I'd check before committing or renewing
Your trailing 12-month income average. Before evaluating affordability, add up your gross client payments over the last year and divide by 12. If that average is comfortably above $7,000/month, your rent is well within range. If it's closer to $4,000–$5,000, it may be stretching you.
Your tax reserve discipline. I see you're already using an Ally Tax Reserve account and made a $4,200 estimated tax payment in April — that's exactly the right instinct. As a Schedule C filer, you'll owe self-employment tax (15.3% on net earnings) plus federal income tax. Before evaluating rent affordability, make sure you're setting aside 25–30% of every client payment before counting it as spendable income.
Your cash runway. A useful freelancer benchmark is 3–6 months of fixed expenses held in liquid savings before committing to a lease. Your fixed monthly costs (rent, healthcare, internet, phone, WeWork, recurring software) run roughly $3,085/month. That means a solid buffer would be $9,255–$18,510 sitting in accessible savings. Do you currently have that cushion?
Your current healthcare cost. At $415/month for your Marketplace premium, that's a significant fixed obligation. Combined with rent, you're at $2,515/month before any other essential spending — worth factoring into your affordability baseline.
Landlord income documentation. Austin landlords typically want to see 3× the monthly rent in verifiable income, or about $6,300/month. For freelancers, they'll usually ask for 2 years of tax returns or bank statements. If your documented income is lumpy, having 2–3 months of rent pre-paid or a larger deposit ready can strengthen your position significantly.
Your rent is defensible if your annual average income is solid — the $13,960 May suggests your earning power is there. The bigger question is whether your cash reserves match the unpredictability of your income stream.
Would you like to work through what a realistic monthly budget looks like using a more conservative income assumption — say, your worst-case monthly average — to see how much cushion you're actually working with?
